summary:
The Kushite 25th Dynasty of Egypt (c. 747–656 BC) was a remarkable episode in which kings from the [[event:1157|Kingdom of Meroe]] Kushite kingdom of Nubia (modern Sudan) conquered and ruled Egypt — reversing the centuries-old pattern of Egyptian domination of Nubia. The Kushite kings, who controlled the gold-rich lands of the upper Nile and had absorbed Egyptian religious and artistic traditions deeply, presented themselves as more authentically Egyptian than the fractured Delta rulers they displaced, restoring temples and reviving archaic Egyptian artistic styles.

The 25th Dynasty kings — Kashta, Piye, Shabaka, Shebitku, Taharqa, and Tantamani — ruled from Memphis and Thebes and made substantial religious investments: Taharqa in particular built extensively at Karnak and throughout Egypt and Nubia, creating monuments that blend Egyptian and Kushite artistic elements. Their rule coincided with Assyrian expansion westward: Taharqa fought the Assyrians three times and was driven back to Nubia by Esarhaddon (671 BC) and Ashurbanipal (667 BC). The forced withdrawal ended the Kushite dynasty in Egypt, but Kushite rulers continued to reign at Napata and later Meroe for another thousand years, developing the distinctive Meroitic civilization that synthesized Pharaonic and African traditions.
